frameworks.name: deep recurrent neural network
frameworks.one_line: Deep RNN learns low-dimensional nonlinear stochastic dynamics from spike trains

frameworks.name: low-dimensional latent dynamical systems
frameworks.one_line: Population activity is summarized by a low-D nonlinear stochastic flow field

frameworks.name: attractor dynamics
frameworks.one_line: Flow fields and attractor structures are read out from the learned latents

frameworks.name: unsupervised representation learning
frameworks.one_line: FINDR infers dynamics without explicit task-variable supervision

core_question: Can an unsupervised deep RNN infer interpretable low-dimensional nonlinear stochastic dynamics underlying neural population activity?
key_assumptions: Population activity arises from low-dimensional nonlinear stochastic dynamics
Task-relevant and task-irrelevant components are separable in the latent space
Spike-train likelihoods constrain the inferred flow field
manipulations: Applied FINDR to frontal-region spike trains during an auditory decision task in rats
Compared FINDR against existing population-dynamics inference methods
Trained variants that disentangle task-relevant vs task-irrelevant latent components
key_findings: FINDR outperformed existing methods in capturing heterogeneous single-neuron responses
Disentangled training recovered interpretable low-dimensional dynamics
Learned low-D latents permitted explicit visualization of flow fields and attractors
interpretations: Unsupervised deep recurrent inference can recover interpretable latent dynamics of decision-related populations
Low-dimensional nonlinear stochastic flow fields are a useful descriptor of frontal-cortex computation
